title: Binary Search
summary: Binary search is two skills wearing one name: the sorted-array lookup you already know, and **binary-search-on-answer** — guess a candidate answer, check feasibility in $O(n)$, and let monotonicity discard half the guesses. The second one is the highest-leverage pattern in your OA prep: "minimize the maximum" problems at Codility and Flipkart are this template verbatim.

notes.heading: Bigger than "find x in a sorted array"
notes.body:
You already know the classic version: sorted array, chop the range in half, $O(\log n)$. In an OA that version is a warm-up at best. The version that actually wins you points is **binary search on a monotone predicate** — and it is the single pattern in this module you have never drilled, which is exactly why it is marked *attack*.

Forget arrays for a second. All binary search really needs is a yes/no question `feasible(x)` whose answers, laid out across the search space, look like `false false ... false true true ... true` (or the mirror image). One flip, and it never flips back. If you can (1) frame the problem as *finding that flip point* and (2) answer `feasible(x)` cheaply, you get a logarithmic algorithm — even when there is no sorted array anywhere in sight.

[[fig:m04-monotone-boundary]]

> [!KEY] Binary search needs monotonicity, not a sorted array
> If `x` works, everything on one side of `x` must also work. A sorted array is just the special case where the predicate is `a[i] >= target`. Once you see it this way, "binary search" becomes a tool for *optimization* problems, not just lookup.

Reach for this module's tools when you see any of these signals:

- The input is **sorted** or **rotated-sorted**, or you can define a monotonic predicate over it.
- You are asked for the **min/max value satisfying a condition** — "minimum capacity", "smallest divisor", "least time to finish", "maximum you can guarantee".
- The answer lives in a numeric range `[lo, hi]`, and a candidate `x` can be **checked in $O(n)$** even though computing the answer directly is expensive.
- Brute force is $O(n^2)$ or $O(n \cdot \text{range})$ and TLEs, but the search space is monotone.

> [!EXAM] The magic phrases
> "**Minimize the maximum** ..." and "**maximize the minimum** ..." are near-certain binary-search-on-answer (parametric search). Codility and Flipkart both love this family — every 🎯 problem in this ladder is the *same template* with a different `feasible` function.

notes.heading: Template 1 — exact match (the dialect you know)
Template 2 — lower_bound (learn this one cold)
Template 3 — binary search on the answer (the money pattern)
When sortedness breaks: rotated arrays
notes.body:
Rotated sorted arrays (`[4,5,6,7,0,1,2]`) look like they kill binary search: the global "sorted" invariant is gone, so `a[mid] < target ⇒ go right` is simply wrong. The rescue is a *local* invariant that survives rotation: **at any `mid`, at least one of the two halves `[lo..mid]` and `[mid..hi]` is fully sorted**, because the rotation point lives in only one of them.

[[fig:m04-rotated-halves]]

One comparison — `a[lo] <= a[mid]` — tells you which half is the sorted one. In a sorted half, a plain range check `a[lo] <= target && target < a[mid]` decides whether the target *can* live there. So each step you identify the sorted half, do the range check, and discard the half you can prove is target-free. Same halving, same $O(\log n)$, just a smarter branch. The full code is in the deep dive below — try the problem first.

> [!KEY] The transferable lesson
> When a global invariant breaks, hunt for a local one you can branch on. "One half is always sorted" is to rotated arrays what "the boundary is monotone" is to on-answer problems.

> [!WARNING] Duplicates change the game
> With duplicates (LC 81, *Search in Rotated Sorted Array II*), `a[lo] == a[mid] == a[hi]` makes the sorted-half test ambiguous — you must shrink with `lo++` and the worst case degrades to $O(n)$. Say this out loud if an interviewer asks the follow-up; it is a known trap, not a trick.

notes.heading: Pitfalls that eat OA points
notes.body:
Binary search is short enough that graders expect it flawless. These are the exact failure modes to sweep for before you hit submit:

- **Overflow:** `mid = lo + (hi - lo) / 2`, always. In on-answer problems make `lo`, `hi`, and any running sums `long long` — a sum of 32-bit ints overflows fast (Split Array's `hi = sum(nums)` is the classic case).
- **`<` vs `<=`:** exact match = inclusive `hi = n - 1` with `while (lo <= hi)`. Boundary / on-answer = exclusive `hi = n` (or the value upper bound) with `while (lo < hi)`. Two dialects, never mixed in one function.
- **Bounds sanity:** for on-answer, check that the true answer really lies in `[lo, hi]`. In Split Array, `lo = max(nums)` — starting at 0 still works but wastes iterations; starting *too high* silently skips the answer.
- **Predicate first:** a wrong `feasible` is invisible inside a correct search. Test it alone (see the OA tip above).

> [!PITFALL] The infinite-loop trap
> In the `lo < hi` style, `hi = mid` + `lo = mid + 1` is the safe pair. The moment you write `if (feasible) lo = mid;` (the "find largest" flip), you MUST bias the midpoint up: `mid = lo + (hi - lo + 1) / 2`. Otherwise, when `hi - lo == 1`, `mid` computes to `lo`, feasible sets `lo = mid = lo`, and nothing ever changes. This single off-by-one is the most common binary-search TLE in existence.

> [!EXAM] The 15-second narration that scores points
> Say it in this order: "The answer is monotone — if X works, X+1 works — so I'll binary search the answer. My feasibility check is [this greedy], $O(n)$. Bounds are [lo, hi]. Total $O(n \log \text{range})$." That sentence signals instant pattern recognition, which is exactly what interviewers and OA graders reward.

problems.name: Binary Search
problems.note: The canonical exact-match template — write it bug-free from memory before anything else.

problems.name: First Bad Version
problems.note: First `true` in false...true — the predicate mindset with minimal code.

problems.name: Sqrt(x)
problems.note: Binary search on the answer value; watch for overflow in `mid*mid` — go `long long`.

problems.name: Find First and Last Position of Element in Sorted Array
problems.note: `lower_bound` + `upper_bound` combined — two boundary searches, zero linear scans.

problems.name: Search in Rotated Sorted Array
problems.note: Decide which half is sorted, range-check it, discard the other — see the deep dive.

problems.name: Find Minimum in Rotated Sorted Array
problems.note: Find the pivot with a monotone comparison of `a[mid]` against `a[hi]`.

problems.name: Koko Eating Bananas
problems.note: The cleanest intro to search-on-answer: smallest speed whose total hours fit in `h`.

problems.name: Capacity To Ship Packages Within D Days
problems.note: Minimize the max daily load — near-identical to Koko with a greedy day counter.

problems.name: Split Array Largest Sum
problems.note: "Minimize the maximum subarray sum" — the archetype OA problem; full walkthrough in the deep dive.

problems.name: Minimum Number of Days to Make m Bouquets
problems.note: Binary search on days; feasibility is a greedy scan counting adjacent bloomed flowers.

problems.name: Find the Smallest Divisor Given a Threshold
problems.note: Monotone sum-of-ceilings predicate — bigger divisor, smaller sum.

problems.name: Median of Two Sorted Arrays
problems.note: Partition-based binary search on the smaller array — see the deep dive.

problems.name: Kth Smallest Element in a Sorted Matrix
problems.note: Binary search on the *value* range; predicate = count of elements ≤ mid via a staircase walk.

deepdives.q: **Split Array Largest Sum** — split `nums` into `k` contiguous subarrays so that the largest subarray sum is as small as possible; return that minimum possible largest sum.
**Search in Rotated Sorted Array** — a sorted array of distinct values was rotated at an unknown pivot; find the index of `target` in $O(\log n)$, or return -1.
**Median of Two Sorted Arrays** — given two sorted arrays of sizes `m` and `n`, return the median of their combined sorted order in $O(\log(\min(m,n)))$.

quiz.q: An OA asks: "Ship packages in the given order within `D` days, minimizing the maximum weight carried on any single day." Which approach fits best?
options: Sliding window over the weights
DP over (package index, days used) — it is the only correct approach
Binary search on the daily capacity, with a greedy feasibility check that counts days
Sort the packages descending and assign with two pointers
quiz.explanation: "Minimize the maximum" + a cheap monotone check ("does capacity X fit in ≤ D days?") is textbook binary-search-on-answer: $O(n \log \text{sum})$. The DP (option B) is *correct* but slower and fiddlier — it is not the *only* correct approach. Sorting (option D) is illegal because packages must ship in order, and a sliding window doesn't optimize a capacity at all.

quiz.q: Inside `while (lo < hi)` with `mid = lo + (hi - lo) / 2`, you write `if (feasible(mid)) hi = mid; else lo = mid;`. What happens?
options: It works — both branches shrink the range
Infinite loop when `hi - lo == 1` and `mid` is infeasible
It returns the answer minus one
It fails only when the array has an even number of elements
quiz.explanation: When `hi - lo == 1`, `mid` computes to `lo`. If `feasible(mid)` is false, `lo = mid` assigns `lo` to itself — the range never shrinks and the loop spins forever. The safe pair in this dialect is `hi = mid` with `lo = mid + 1`; if you genuinely need `lo = mid` (find-largest version), you must bias the midpoint up: `mid = lo + (hi - lo + 1) / 2`.

quiz.q: Koko-style search-on-answer: `n` piles, largest pile `M`, and each feasibility check scans all piles once. Total time complexity?
options: $O(n \log n)$
$O(\log M)$
$O(n \cdot M)$
$O(n \log M)$
quiz.explanation: The binary search runs over the *value range* `[1, M]`, taking $O(\log M)$ iterations, and each iteration pays an $O(n)$ feasibility scan — $O(n \log M)$ total. $O(n \log n)$ confuses the value range with the array size; $O(n \cdot M)$ is the brute force that trying every speed would cost.

quiz.q: Using the boundary template, what does `lowerBound(a, 5)` return for `a = [1, 3, 5, 5, 5, 8]`?
quiz.explanation: `lower_bound` returns the *first* index with `a[i] >= 5`. The run of 5s starts at index 2, so the answer is 2. Index 4 is the *last* 5 (that boundary is `upper_bound - 1`), index 1 holds 3 (< 5), and 6 = `n` would mean no element ≥ 5 exists.

quiz.q: Why write `mid = lo + (hi - lo) / 2` instead of `mid = (lo + hi) / 2`?
options: It is faster on modern CPUs
It rounds toward `hi` instead of `lo`
It is required for the `while (lo < hi)` dialect to terminate
It avoids signed integer overflow when `lo + hi` exceeds `INT_MAX`
quiz.explanation: Both formulas compute the same value mathematically, but `lo + hi` can overflow a 32-bit int when both are large (huge arrays, or on-answer bounds like `sum(nums)`), producing a negative `mid` and undefined behavior. `lo + (hi - lo) / 2` never exceeds `hi`. It has nothing to do with rounding direction or termination.

quiz.q: In rotated-array search (no duplicates), the check `a[lo] <= a[mid]` succeeding guarantees exactly what?
options: The target lies in the left half
The subarray `[lo..mid]` is fully sorted
The subarray `[mid..hi]` contains the rotation pivot... and the target
`mid` is the rotation pivot
quiz.explanation: The rotation pivot lives in exactly one half, and `a[lo] <= a[mid]` proves it is not in `[lo..mid]` — so that half is fully sorted. It says *nothing* about where the target is; you still need the range check `a[lo] <= target && target < a[mid]` to decide which half to keep. The pivot being in the right half doesn't mean the target is (option C).

quiz.q: In Split Array Largest Sum, why is `lo = max(nums)` the correct lower bound for the search?
options: Some piece must contain the largest element, so no cap below `max(nums)` is ever feasible
It guarantees the greedy produces exactly `k` pieces
Without it, the sum overflows 32-bit int
It is what makes `feasible` monotonic
quiz.explanation: Whatever split you choose, the piece holding the largest element has sum ≥ `max(nums)`, so any cap below that is infeasible — the answer can't be lower. Starting lower (0 or 1) still returns the right answer, just with wasted iterations; monotonicity comes from the greedy's structure, not the bound; and overflow is handled by `long long`, not by `lo`.

quiz.q: You need the LARGEST `x` with `feasible(x)` true (pattern `true...true, false...false`), so you write `if (feasible(mid)) lo = mid; else hi = mid - 1;`. Which midpoint must you use?
options: `mid = lo + (hi - lo) / 2` — the standard one
`mid = hi - (hi - lo) / 2` — bias toward hi is automatic
`mid = lo + (hi - lo + 1) / 2` — biased up, or the loop hangs when `hi - lo == 1`
Any of them — the branch logic alone guarantees progress
quiz.explanation: With `lo = mid` in play, the standard down-biased mid equals `lo` when `hi - lo == 1`; if `feasible(lo)` is true you assign `lo = lo` forever. The `+1` biases `mid` up to `hi` in that state, so either `lo` jumps to `hi` or `hi` drops below — progress either way. Option B is the same value as option A in disguise for this case, and option D is exactly the classic infinite-loop bug.

m04-monotone-boundary.caption: The shape every binary search exploits: `feasible(x)` is **false** for a while, then **true** forever. Binary search finds the flip point — the smallest feasible value — in $O(\log)$ probes.

m04-rotated-halves.caption: `[4,5,6,7,0,1,2]` with `mid` at index 3: comparing `a[lo]=4 ≤ a[mid]=7` proves the left half is sorted, so a simple range check decides which half to discard. The pivot drop (7 → 0) always hides in exactly one half.

m04-median-partition.caption: Median of two sorted arrays: cut `A` after `i = 2` elements and `B` after `j = half − i = 3`. The partition is valid when both **cross-checks** hold (`A[i−1] ≤ B[j]` and `B[j−1] ≤ A[i]`); then the median comes from the four boundary values only.
